content.text: Immutable build artifacts are packaged software units that are created exactly once and then deployed verbatim to every environment without modification. The core principle is
: the binary that passes tests in the development environment is the same binary — not a rebuilt copy, but the identical bytes — that runs in staging and production.
Immutability is enforced through content-addressable identifiers. A container image is referred to by its SHA256 digest (
) rather than a mutable tag such as

. The digest is a cryptographic hash of the image contents; any change to any byte produces a different digest. Once the registry stores the image, it cannot be altered. Deployment tooling references the digest, guaranteeing that what was built is precisely what runs.

For agentic AI services, immutability extends beyond application code to the full agentic assembly: the system prompt text, the tool schema definitions, and the model version reference are all frozen at build time and bundled into the artifact alongside the orchestration code. This is what separates a genuine immutable artifact for an agent from a container image that merely holds code but reads its prompts from a mutable database at runtime.

Agentic systems are sensitive to configuration in ways that conventional services are not. A change to the system prompt can shift the model's behavior as dramatically as a code change — and sometimes more so. If the system prompt is read from a database at runtime rather than baked into the artifact, then two deployments of the same container image may behave differently depending on which database row is current. This makes test results from staging unreliable as predictors of production behavior.

The "works on my machine" problem, long familiar in software development, reappears in agentic AI as "passes in staging but behaves differently in production." Immutable artifacts eliminate this class of problem by construction: if the artifact is identical, the only remaining variable is the environment it runs in, which can be controlled and verified separately.
Any environment running the same artifact digest exhibits identical code and configuration behavior. Test results transfer across environment boundaries.
Rolling back to a previous version means deploying an artifact that already ran in production and was verified. There is no need to rebuild from source under incident pressure.
Every production deployment's exact state — including system prompt text and model reference — can be reconstructed from the artifact digest.
Signed artifacts with SBOM attachments prove provenance: this binary was built from this source commit at this time and has not been modified since.
Deployment is reduced to "point the environment at this digest." No build steps, no configuration merging, no risk of a partial deployment.
Multiple teams can promote different artifact versions through different environment tracks without risk of overwriting each other.

Strict immutability means that even a one-line change to a system prompt requires a full rebuild and re-promotion through the pipeline. For teams that iterate rapidly on prompts this can be friction. The solution is usually to tier configuration: frozen-in-artifact for production-critical settings, runtime-configurable for low-risk parameters like log level or feature flags.
Retaining every artifact version indefinitely accumulates storage. Establish a retention policy: keep the last N versions plus any version currently deployed in any environment.
Rebuilding the entire container for a prompt change is slower than editing a database row. Layer caching and multi-stage Docker builds minimize this cost.
Model version pinning adds upgrade friction.

Pinning the model version in the artifact means upgrading to a new model release requires a rebuild and re-promotion cycle, even if no code changed. This is intentional — model upgrades carry behavioral risk — but must be managed.

At build time, the CI pipeline assembles all components into a single OCI-compliant container image or artifact bundle. The assembly includes: the application code and dependencies, the system prompt files, the tool schema JSON, and a configuration file specifying the model version identifier. The image is pushed to a container registry, which computes and returns the SHA256 digest. No subsequent step may modify the image contents; any change produces a new digest and thus a new artifact.

Artifact signing adds a cryptographic proof of origin. Tools such as Cosign (from the Sigstore project) sign the image digest with a key tied to the CI pipeline identity. Downstream deployment systems verify the signature before pulling the image, ensuring that images not signed by the trusted build system are rejected.

A Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) is attached to the artifact at build time, listing every dependency including Python packages, prompt file versions, and the model identifier. The SBOM enables security scanning against known vulnerability databases and provides a complete audit record of the artifact's composition.

attrs.alt: Build once, deploy everywhere: a single build produces a container image, prompt bundle, and model reference; these are signed and pushed to an immutable artifact registry; the same digest is pulled to dev, staging, and production. What lives inside the artifact (code, prompts, tool schema, locked dependencies) versus what stays outside (secrets, feature flags, scale config) is illustrated.

content.text: The left panel shows what is baked into the artifact at build time: the container image (digest-pinned), the prompt bundle (version-tagged), and the model reference (exact version string). The registry stores these immutably. The right panel shows what the artifact

contain: secrets, feature flag states, database connection strings, and environment-specific scale settings. These belong in environment configuration stores (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Kubernetes ConfigMaps) and are injected at runtime — but only after the artifact's integrity has been verified.
for any agent service where staging test results are expected to predict production behavior. This is the correct default for all production agent deployments.
(all prompts and model references baked in) for agents handling high-stakes actions: financial transactions, customer data writes, external API calls with side effects.
only for low-risk cosmetic parameters (e.g., language locale) that do not affect the agent's reasoning or action selection, and where the configuration change is itself gated and audited.
only for rapid-iteration developer tools where deployment speed matters more than staging-to-production reproducibility. Reintroduce immutability before external or enterprise-scale deployment.
Move all prompt files into the source repository under a
file that specifies the exact model version string (e.g.,
both the prompt files and the model-config.json into the image at build time.
Configure the CI pipeline to build and push the image, capturing the registry-returned digest.
Sign the image with Cosign using the OIDC keyless signing workflow, tying the signature to the CI pipeline identity.
Generate an SBOM with Syft and attach it to the image in the registry.
Update all deployment manifests to reference the image by digest, not by tag.
Dockerfile — Baking Prompts and Model Config

# Dockerfile for an agentic service with baked-in prompts and model config
FROM python:3.12-slim AS base

WORKDIR /app

# Install Python dependencies from a lockfile so the dependency tree is frozen
COPY requirements.lock ./
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.lock

# Copy application code
COPY src/ ./src/

# Bake prompts and model configuration into the image at build time.
# These files are now immutable inside the container — no runtime reads from
# a database or mounted volume can change them unless the image is rebuilt.
COPY prompts/ ./prompts/
COPY model-config.json ./model-config.json
COPY tools/schemas/ ./tools/schemas/

# Record the git SHA and build time for traceability
ARG GIT_SHA=unknown
ARG BUILD_TIME=unknown
ENV GIT_SHA=${GIT_SHA} BUILD_TIME=${BUILD_TIME}

USER 1000
ENTRYPOINT ["python", "-m", "src.agent"]
CI Pipeline — Capture and Sign Digest

# .github/workflows/build.yml
name: Build Immutable Artifact

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  contents: read
  id-token: write  # Required for Cosign OIDC keyless signing
  packages: write

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      image-digest: ${{ steps.build.outputs.digest }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Login to GHCR
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Build and push (digest captured automatically)
        id: build
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          context: .
          push: true
          tags: ghcr.io/myorg/agent:${{ github.sha }}
          build-args: |
            GIT_SHA=${{ github.sha }}
            BUILD_TIME=${{ github.event.head_commit.timestamp }}
          cache-from: type=gha
          cache-to: type=gha,mode=max

      - name: Sign image with Cosign (keyless)
        uses: sigstore/cosign-installer@v3
      - run: |
          cosign sign --yes \
            ghcr.io/myorg/agent@${{ steps.build.outputs.digest }}

      - name: Generate and attach SBOM
        uses: anchore/sbom-action@v0
        with:
          image: ghcr.io/myorg/agent@${{ steps.build.outputs.digest }}
          artifact-name: sbom.spdx.json
          output-file: sbom.spdx.json

      - name: Store digest for downstream jobs
        run: echo "IMAGE_DIGEST=${{ steps.build.outputs.digest }}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

A financial services team built an agent that drafts customer account summaries. Initially, system prompts were stored in a PostgreSQL table so product managers could edit them without a deployment. After a PM accidentally modified the prompt for a production audience while testing a new phrasing idea, the agent began generating factually incorrect summaries for several hours before the issue was noticed in monitoring.
The team moved all prompts into the source repository under

with semantic versioning. Every prompt change now goes through a PR, triggers a full pipeline, and must pass the staging eval gate before reaching production. The PM editing workflow moved to a prompt preview environment that builds a one-off artifact from a feature branch. The database table was retired. Since the change, no prompt-related production incident has occurred.
means the image can change between a deployment command and the pod starting. Always deploy by digest.
Mounting prompt files from a ConfigMap or reading them from a database at startup defeats the immutability guarantee. Any mutable runtime input is a source of environment divergence.
(without the date suffix) means the model the artifact uses can change without a rebuild if the provider updates what that alias resolves to.
Generating an SBOM but never querying it for vulnerabilities provides no real security benefit. Integrate SBOM scanning into the promotion gate.
Retaining every artifact indefinitely grows storage costs. Retain all currently-deployed artifacts plus a configurable history window (e.g., last 30 days).
) for prompt directories. A patch bump indicates a wording fix, a minor bump a new capability, a major bump a fundamental behavioral change.
Reference images by digest in all manifests.
Kubernetes Deployments, Helm charts, and Terraform resources should always use
Sign artifacts in CI, verify in deployment.
Use Cosign's keyless signing workflow in CI and an admission controller (Sigstore Policy Controller or OPA/Gatekeeper) to verify signatures before pods start.
Include a manifest.json inside the artifact.
An in-artifact manifest records the prompt bundle version, model reference, and git SHA. The running agent exposes this at a
endpoint, enabling instant traceability in production.
Cache Python dependency installation across builds. A prompt-only change should not trigger a full pip install.
An immutable artifact is like a

. The drug is manufactured once in a controlled environment, tested thoroughly, sealed with a tamper-evident cap, and assigned a lot number. From that moment, the vial's contents cannot be changed — only its location changes as it moves from the factory to the warehouse to the pharmacy to the patient. The lot number lets anyone trace exactly where and when this specific batch was made and which quality tests it passed.

Open a pharmaceutical vial to "improve the formula" after quality testing is a serious violation. Editing a production Docker image after it passes staging — even to fix a typo in a prompt — is the software equivalent. Rebuild, test, and re-promote a new sealed artifact instead.

Arrange the Dockerfile so that infrequently-changed layers (OS packages, Python dependencies) come before frequently-changed layers (source code, prompts). A prompt-only change should rebuild only the last one or two layers.

Large images slow down pull times during deployment. Use multi-stage builds to exclude build tools from the final image. A minimal agent image might be 150–250 MB; a naive image with build tools can exceed 2 GB.
If the agent runs on both x86_64 and ARM (e.g., EC2 Graviton), use Docker Buildx with multi-platform builds to produce a single manifest list covering both architectures from the same build step.
The standard container build tool. BuildKit adds parallel layer builds, cache mounts, and multi-platform support.
The specification that defines the image format and distribution protocol. All major registries (ECR, GCR, GHCR, Docker Hub, Harbor) are OCI-compliant.
Tool for signing and verifying container image digests. Supports keyless signing tied to OIDC identities (GitHub Actions, GCP Workload Identity).
Generates SBOMs in SPDX or CycloneDX format from container images or source directories.
Vulnerability scanners that accept SBOM or container image input and check against CVE databases.
Kubernetes admission controller that enforces image signature verification before allowing pods to start.
Digest verification prevents image substitution attacks.
Deploying by SHA256 digest rather than tag means a compromised registry cannot serve a different image under the same tag.
An attacker who modifies an image after signing produces a digest that does not match the signature; the deployment verification step rejects it.
Keep secrets out of the image.
API keys, database passwords, and model API credentials must never be baked into the artifact. Use environment variables injected from Vault or Secrets Manager at runtime.
Scan the SBOM on every promotion.
A vulnerability discovered in a dependency after the image was built should trigger a gate failure for any promotion attempt, even if the image itself has not changed.
Immutable build artifacts are created once and deployed verbatim to every environment; the SHA256 digest is the cryptographic proof of identity.
For agentic AI, immutability must extend to system prompts, tool schema definitions, and model version references — not just application code.
Deploy by digest, not by mutable tag; a mutable tag can change between the deploy command and the pod starting.
Sign artifacts with Cosign and verify signatures at deployment time to prevent post-build tampering and image substitution attacks.
Keep secrets outside the artifact; only code, prompts, and configuration that is identical across environments belongs inside.
Layer caching and multi-stage builds minimize the build overhead of rebuilding the image for a prompt-only change.
An in-artifact manifest.json exposed at a /version endpoint provides instant runtime traceability of which prompt bundle and model version is running in any environment.
